title: That LastPass or Bitwarden Security Email May Be a Scam
body:
Emily Long
                            
        
            Emily Long
                
                                                                        Freelance Writer
                                                            
            
            
        
            ExperienceEmily Long is a freelance writer based in Salt Lake City. After graduating from Duke University, she spent several years reporting on the federal workforce for Government Executive, a publication of Atlantic Media Company, in Washington, D.C. She has nearly a decade of experience as a freelancer covering tech (including issues related to security, privacy, and streaming) as well as personal finance and travel. In addition to Lifehacker, her work has been featured on Wirecutter, Tom’s Guide, and ZDNET. Emily has also worked as a travel guide around the U.S. and as a content editor. She has a masters in social work and is a licensed therapist in Utah.
        
        
            Areas of Expertise
        
        Read Full Bio
    

    
                                        
        
        
        July 17, 2026
                
        Add as a preferred source on Google

    
    
        Add as a preferred source on Google

            Credit: Zooey Liao/Lifehacker/Getty Images
                            
    Table of Contents
    
        
        You trust your password manager to keep your credentials, documents, and identity data secure—and you probably also trust notices that come from your password manager with steps to maintain that security. Scammers are counting on this: A new phishing campaign is targeting LastPass and Bitwarden users with fake security alerts designed to compromise their data and devices. Earlier this week, LastPass alerted users to an impersonation scam in which threat actors are sending phishing emails that look like official security notices. The messages come from hello[at]lastpassnewsletter[.]com with the subject line "Action Required: Review Updated LastPass Security Policies." In the email body, attackers outlined supposed changes to LastPass security monitoring and reporting protocols and noted that users "have 14 business days to review and accept the updated terms" via DocuSign. The link redirected to https[:]//lastpasscompliance[.]com/, which looked like a legitimate DocuSign page complete with a chatbot window, and prompted users to "download" DocuSign in order to review and sign the document. It's unclear whether the goal was to spread malware or harvest user credentials, as the malicious website has since been taken down. However, Bitwarden users have been targeted with a nearly identical campaign, according to BleepingComputer. 
How to spot the password manager scamOn the surface, the phishing emails targeting LastPass and Bitwarden users are pretty convincing. They have some technical jargon, so users may skim over the specifics and trust that the information is legitimate. There's a call to action, but the email states that users have 14 days to accept the terms or their account "may be temporarily restricted"—so the urgency is slightly lower than with some other scams. The email even reassures users that their vaults and accounts are "completely secure" and states that the required steps are "strictly" administrative in nature.  
            
                What do you think so far?
            
        
That said, both the sender and the URL should raise suspicion. Neither lastpassnewsletter[.]com nor lastpasscompliance[.]com are official LastPass domains, nor is bitwardencompliance[.]com a real Bitwarden site. Never enter your master password or other credentials unless you navigate directly to your password manager's website or vault—links from emails, texts, or social media messages are at risk of being phishing attempts. If you've supplied your credentials to a suspicious site, update them immediately from a trusted device. You also shouldn't need to download software or use DocuSign for your password manager, and any actions on your account should occur when you are logged in to the legitimate site or vault. 
            
    
        
                
                    
                Lifehacker has been a go-to source of tech help and life advice since 2005. Our mission is to offer reliable tech help and credible, practical, science-based life advice to help you live better.
                
                
                    
                        Our Mission
                        Our Team
                        Newsletter
                    
                
            
        
                © 2001-2026 Ziff Davis, LLC., A ZIFF DAVIS COMPANY. ALL RIGHTS RESERVED.
                Lifehacker is a federally registered trademark of Ziff Davis and may not be used by third parties without explicit permission. The display of third-party trademarks and trade names on this site does not necessarily indicate
                    any affiliation or the
                    endorsement of Lifehacker. If you click an affiliate link and buy a product or service, we may be paid a fee by that merchant.

schema.headline: That LastPass or Bitwarden Security Email May Be a Scam

title: Lifehacker's Big Guessing Game Is in Its Second Round
body:
Jake Peterson
                            
        
            Jake Peterson
                
                                            Senior Technology Editor
                                    
            
            
        
            ExperienceJake Peterson is Lifehacker’s Tech Editor, and has been covering tech news and how-tos for nearly a decade. His team covers all things technology, including AI, smartphones, computers, game consoles, and subscriptions.
        
        
            Areas of Expertise
        
        Read Full Bio
    

    
                                        
        
        
        July 22, 2026
                
        Add as a preferred source on Google

    
    
        Add as a preferred source on Google

            Credit: Rene Ramos/Lifehacker
                            
    Table of Contents
    
        
        Update: The second round of the Big Guessing Game contest has ended. Please come back on Aug. 18 for the third round.Attention Apple fans: If you make a habit of following the leaks and rumors concerning Apple's upcoming product announcements, you're in luck. Lifehacker, in partnership with the other CNET Group sites, is hosting the second round of its Big Guessing Game. By making your guesses, you'll put yourself in the running to win a brand-new Apple Watch.  If you already participated in Round 1, you know how this works. For new players, here's the gist: Lifehacker is running a guessing game throughout 2026, inviting readers to make predictions about what Apple may be announcing this year. The contest has three rounds.
Round 1, which ended on June 2. This round was focused on Apple's software announcements (iOS, iPadOS, macOS, watchOS, visionOS, etc.).Round 2, which will run from July 7 through July 21. This round has questions about Apple hardware (iPhones, iPads, Macs, Apple Watches, Vision Pro and so on.).Round 3, which will span Aug. 18 through Sept. 1. This round focuses on Apple's fall event.Each round has five questions about what Apple may reveal during its keynotes and announcements. We won't know the answers until Apple confirms them, but each correct answer earns you one entry into the grand-prize drawing. CNET Group will hold this drawing at the end of the third round. If you win that drawing, you'll receive the latest version of an Apple Watch.    To be eligible, an entrant must be at least 18 years old, and a resident of the 50 U.S. states or Washington, D.C. You don't just have to play Lifehacker's guessing game, either: All CNET Group sites (CNET, Mashable, PCMag, ZDNET) are also hosting their own questions, which means there will be 75 possible queries to answer across all CNET Group sites and all three rounds. You can answer any or all of the questions on these sites during each round, but note that each entrant is only eligible to win one prize. Each of the five websites will announce the winner of its contest shortly after Apple's fall event.
            
                What do you think so far?
            
        
NO PURCHASE NECESSARY to enter or win the "CNET Group Big Guessing Game" Giveaway. Open to legal U.S. residents in the 50 U.S. & D.C., 18+ yrs of age. Other restrictions apply. Begins May 19, 2026 at 12:01 p.m. ET and ends Sept. 1, 2026 at 11:59 p.m. ET. Void where prohibited. Subject to Official Rules. Sponsor: Ziff Davis, LLC.Apple is not a sponsor of, affiliated with, or endorser of this sweepstakes. Apple Watch is a trademark of Apple Inc.

schema.headline: Lifehacker's Big Guessing Game Is in Its Second Round

title: iOS 26.6 Comes With Two New Features, and One Is Key for iOS 27
body:
Jake Peterson
                            
        
            Jake Peterson
                
                                            Senior Technology Editor
                                    
            
            
        
            ExperienceJake Peterson is Lifehacker’s Tech Editor, and has been covering tech news and how-tos for nearly a decade. His team covers all things technology, including AI, smartphones, computers, game consoles, and subscriptions.
        
        
            Areas of Expertise
        
        Read Full Bio
    

    
                                        
        
        
        July 21, 2026
                
        Add as a preferred source on Google

    
    
        Add as a preferred source on Google

            Credit: Thomas Trutschel / Contributor via Getty Images
                            
            
                Key Takeaways
                                            Apple's next update isn't iOS 27: it's iOS 26.6.
                                            The update comes with two features you need to know about, one of which makes it possible to use Siri AI immediately after installing iOS 27..
                                            The other sends you an alert after you've blocked too many contacts on your iPhone, though that exact limit appears unclear.
                                    
            
    Table of Contents
    
        
        When you think about a big iPhone update on the horizon, iOS 27 likely comes to mind. And yet, Apple is almost ready to release a totally different update: iOS 26.6. Typically, iOS updates this close to the launch of the next major version are focused on security fixes and squashing bugs, saving any major changes for the next big numbered update. And yet, while iOS 26.6 is a decidedly small update, it's still a notable one, deliveringtwo new features you should be aware of—including one that actually lays the groundwork for iOS 27.Still in betaTo be clear, iOS 26.6 isn't actually out yet. Apple seeded the "Release Candidate" (RC) to beta testers this week; this is the version of iOS 26.6 the company intends to ship to the general public. If testers find no issues with the update, we'll be getting this version of iOS 26.6 imminently. Otherwise, Apple may make some adjustments. But regardless, these below features are coming to your iPhone with the next update.
Preparing your iPhone for Siri AIiOS 26.6's first feature won't reveal itself when you install the update on your iPhone. In fact, most of us may think iOS 26.6 has no new features at all.  But the update's most important new change is key for compatible iPhones upgrading to iOS 27. As it turns out, iOS 26.6 "optimizes the Spotlight index" on your iPhone to prepare it for iOS 27. In other words, the update gets your iPhone ready for Siri AI. Apple's AI-powered assistant relies on the new search architecture in iOS 27 to run properly. As such, if you install iOS 27 without updating to iOS 26.6 first, it may take days before Siri AI can perform as expected. By preparing your iPhone with iOS 26.6 now, you'll avoid the growing pains.
            
                What do you think so far?
            
        
Of course, that won't benefit everyone eventually upgrading to iOS 27. While the update runs on the iPhone 12 and newer devices, only the iPhone 15 Pro and newer can try Siri AI. Still, if that's you, iOS 27's new Siri will run much more smoothly coming from iOS 26.6 than from iOS 26.5.2.     iOS 26.6's other new feature is a new alert, letting you know that you've reached iOS' limit of the number of contacts you can block. It's not clear exactly how many contacts that is, but new code in the update confirms the alert is present. The alert reads: "Blocked Contacts Limit Reached: You’ve reached the maximum number of blocked contacts. To block additional callers, remove a blocked contact in Settings."The thing is, there's no documentation of what that limit actually is. In fact, anecdotal evidence suggests that the limit is user-dependent. MacRumors reports that some users receive the alert after blocking 20,000 contacts, while others only get 8,000. I've seen comments suggesting the limit is lower still, with some claiming to hit it in the 5,000s. Nonetheless, that's a lot of blocked contacts, and will likely only apply to users who receive an overwhelming number of calls and messages from unwanted sources.

schema.headline: iOS 26.6 Comes With Two New Features, and One Is Key for iOS 27

title: It's Not Just You: YouTube's Picture-in-Picture Isn't Working
body:
Jake Peterson
                            
        
            Jake Peterson
                
                                            Senior Technology Editor
                                    
            
            
        
            ExperienceJake Peterson is Lifehacker’s Tech Editor, and has been covering tech news and how-tos for nearly a decade. His team covers all things technology, including AI, smartphones, computers, game consoles, and subscriptions.
        
        
            Areas of Expertise
        
        Read Full Bio
    

    
                                        
        
        
        July 20, 2026
                
        Add as a preferred source on Google

    
    
        Add as a preferred source on Google

            Credit: 5./15 WEST via Getty Images
                            
            
                Key Takeaways
                                            As of Monday morning, YouTube's picture-in-picture (PiP) mode is broken for some users.
                                            YouTube has confirmed the issue, and says it is working on a fix.
                                            The issue appears to affect both iOS and Android, but is reportedly much more common on iPhone.
                                    
            
    Table of Contents
    
        
        Picture-in-picture (PiP) is great for us multitaskers. While some prefer to pay full attention to their content, others prefer to keep videos running while attending to other tasks. I frequently have a video playing in PiP on both my iPhone and Mac, while I scan headlines, chat with friends, or take care of any to-do that only requires half my focus. While there are ways to manually trigger PiP, it's best on mobile, as most video player apps have adopted a natural method: When you play a video, you can simply leave the app, and the video continues playing in a PiP window. It's now muscle memory for me on iOS: I head to YouTube, choose a video, and swipe out of the app, knowing my video will keep running as I move to other apps on my iPhone.       YouTube PiP is broken (especially on iOS)Of course, like most things, PiP is only good when it works. You might have recently tried the exact shortcut I just described, only to find that nothing actually happens. You close out of YouTube, but your video doesn't follow in a PiP window. (Though it may still continue to play in the background.) And while some of you might be Android users, I'd be willing to bet most are on iPhones. What gives? 
As reported by 9to5Google, YouTube is currently experiencing issues with PiP. This isn't something only a handful of users are dealing with. The problem is apparently so widespread, YouTube has confirmed the issue directly. In a statement on YouTube Help, a Google employee shared the following:
            
                What do you think so far?
            
        
"We're aware that some of you are experiencing issues with the Picture-in-Picture (PiP) feature failing to trigger when exiting the YouTube app. While the vast majority of these reports are coming from iOS users, we are also seeing a significantly lower volume of reports from Android users...Our teams are actively investigating the issue, and we'll update this thread as soon as we have more information." At the time of writing, over 11,600 users hit the "I have the same question" button on this YouTube Help post. Is there any way to fix PiP in the YouTube app?Unfortunately, it doesn't seem like there is any workaround. For what it's worth, PiP was intermittent for me this morning in the YouTube app on iOS. I had no problem swiping out of a video into PiP mode during an ad, but once the video started playing, PiP didn't work. Not only that, but playback stopped as well. I went back to YouTube, played the video again, and when I left the app this time, PiP kicked in. This continued on and off, not working one time, but working the next.

schema.headline: It's Not Just You: YouTube's Picture-in-Picture Isn't Working

title: Post-Quantum Encryption Could Be the Must-Have VPN Feature of the Future
body:
Quantum computers can't crack regular VPN encryption just yet, but what happens when that changes?
            
                        
    
                            
        
        
        July 15, 2026
            
            
                        Credit: René Ramos/Lifehacker/Adobe Stock
                            
    
        
        Cybersecurity experts warn that about 10-20 years from now, quantum computers will have enough processing power to decipher common cryptography techniques like RSA and ECC, an event they call "Q-Day." These encryption protocols are the current gold standard in VPN tech, but when Q-Day arrives, they’ll no longer be enough. Post-quantum encryption (or PQE), which uses complex mathematical puzzles that even quantum computers struggle with, is your VPN provider’s answer to the Q-Day threat. But while Q-Day is still years away from actualization, PQE is a VPN feature you can enable today, to protect your data against hackers who might hold on to your encrypted data until quantum computers get strong enough to decrypt it. NordVPN, ExpressVPN, Mullvad, and others let you enable PQE right away with the click of a button. But is this future-proof encryption protocol necessary or worth the investment today? I’ll explain how it works, what it protects against, and which providers offer it so you can make up your own mind. How post-quantum encryption (PQE) worksWhen you access the internet through a VPN tunnel, it basically scrambles your traffic data into unreadable code that can only be unlocked using a cipher key. That key is then securely transmitted to your device using a VPN handshake. So anyone who doesn’t have access to the encryption key, including your ISP, will not be able to interpret any useful information from your network and data usage. However, this technology only works because hackers don’t have access to hardware that can decrypt the scrambled network data without the encryption key. With quantum computing evolving as quickly as it is, security researchers estimate that it will be powerful enough to fully decipher your encrypted data without access to the actual key. Q-Day isn’t merely a distant threat. Attackers are already using “harvest now, decrypt later” (HNDL) attacks to hoard your encrypted network data so that they can decrypt it as soon as Q-Day arrives. That means they’ll suddenly gain access to years of private user data the second that quantum computers gain enough processing power through hardware improvements. That’s why in August 2024, the NIST standardized new encryption algorithms from an open competition that ran for years. Three models were found complex enough to resist attacks from quantum computers: ML-KEM (aka Kyber) for key exchange, along with ML-DSA and SLH-DSA for digital signatures.If your work involves sensitive data, if you perform a lot of high-value financial transactions, or if you are in a regulated industry with strict compliance requirements, PQE is worth looking into. While its availability is limited to certain VPN providers on specific pricing tiers only, security-conscious businesses and professionals operating in sensitive industries will find it genuinely useful, so it’s not just a gimmick. VPNs that support post-quantum encryptionA lot of companies use the phrase "quantum-safe" in their marketing terminology, but not all offer PQE as a standard VPN feature. Right now, NordVPN, ExpressVPN, and Mullvad are three mainstream providers who offer PQE as an opt-in feature on several plans. NordVPNNord rolled out PQE in its Linux VPN app in 2024, followed by Windows, Android, and Apple operating systems. It runs on the NordLynx protocol with the ML-KEM algorithm layered on top of the existing Wireguard encryption. But that also means you can’t use PQE with NordVPN if you’re using a dedicated IP, Meshnet, or the OpenVPN protocol. Because PQE is available to all NordLynx users, you don’t need a premium subscription tier or add-on to access it. All existing NordVPN customers can enable it, whether you’re on the Basic plan or Prime. Here’s how to do it if you’re already using NordVPN: On Windows: Open the NordVPN app, go to Settings > Connections. Toggle on Post-quantum encryption.On macOS: Open the app on your Mac device. Go to Settings, then navigate to the General tab and enable the Post-quantum encryption toggle. On iOS, Android, tvOS, or Android TV: Tap your profile icon. From here, you can easily find the toggle for Post-quantum encryption. On Linux: Navigate to terminal. Run this command: nordvpn set pq on. ExpressVPNEven before its NIST standardization, ExpressVPN was one of the first companies to offer the Kyber algorithm through its Lightway protocol as early as 2023. When the ML-KEM protocol was standardized, ExpressVPN upgraded its platform to adhere to the exact NIST standards with several improvements. 
            
                What do you think so far?
            
        
Interestingly, ExpressVPN has PQE enabled on every user plan by default. However, you need to be using the Lightway protocol and not a legacy alternative like OpenVPN. If you aren't sure if PQE is enabled, here’s how to check: Open the ExpressVPN app. Make sure you’re on the latest version of the app.Click on Settings. Navigate to the Protocol tab. Check if one of the following options is selected: Automatic, Lightway UDP, or Lightway TCP. If not, just choose any one of them to enable PQE. MullvadMullvad introduced a pilot version of its post-quantum encryption key exchange protocol way back in 2017, but the experimental implementation was replaced by a new version based on the Classic McEliece algorithm on top of all existing WireGuard servers. With the 2023.3 launch of its desktop app, Mullvad launched a fully stabilized PQE feature while extending support for Android and iOS.Mullvad doesn’t have a feature-based pricing tier to worry about. Every paying customer has access to this feature with no add-on purchases necessary. It should be enabled by default on desktop unless you specifically opted out of it, but a manual opt-in is necessary on smartphone apps. You can do this by following these steps:On Windows, macOS, and Linux: Go to Settings > VPN settings > WireGuard settings. Make sure that the Quantum-resistant tunnel option is switched to On. When the VPN is connected, you should see a string of green text that reads: “QUANTUM SECURE CONNECTION.” On Android and iOS: Launch the Mullvad VPN app. Go to Settings > VPN settings. Find the Quantum-resistant tunnel option, then toggle it on. Does it make sense to use PQE right now?There’s no downside to enabling post-quantum encryption if your VPN provider already supports it on your existing plan. I didn’t notice any meaningful latency or speed drops when testing the feature on NordVPN and ExpressVPN. But if you’re wondering if it’s worth switching to a different plan or provider just for PQE, the answer depends on what your typical workflow looks like. Quantum computers are still years away from being able to decrypt current security protocols like RSA and ECC, even by the most generous estimates. However, HNDL attacks are a big enough security red flag that companies or people operating in sensitive industries and dealing with confidential information should be paying attention. For regular browsing workflows, PQE may be overkill. But when dealing with financial data, accessing healthcare portals, or sending sensitive information that you can’t afford to have intercepted, quantum protection offers additional peace of mind.
            
                        
                
                    
                                            
                
                
                                            
    More by Ritoban

schema.headline: Post-Quantum Encryption Could Be the Must-Have VPN Feature of the Future

title: This Safari Extension Can Block Unwanted Content on Any Website
body:
Pranay Parab
                            
        
            Pranay Parab
                
                                                                        Freelance Writer
                                                            
            
            
        
            ExperiencePranay Parab is an independent tech journalist based in Mumbai, India. He covers tech for Lifehacker, and specializes in tutorials and in-depth features.
        
        
            Areas of Expertise
        
        Read Full Bio
    

    
                                        
        
        
        July 16, 2026
                
        Add as a preferred source on Google

    
    
        Add as a preferred source on Google

            Credit: Pranay Parab
                            
    Table of Contents
    
        
        I try to avoid opening news websites as much as possible these days. One can only read so many articles about crime, war, and all of the many other injustices in this world, and I've often wished I could filter out the news I don't want to know about. I've done this, to a large extent, on social media platforms, some of which allow you to block posts containing certain keywords, while doing so on others requires a browser extension. However, the problem persists on the broader web: I can't open any big-name news website without seeing repeated mentions of the poor decisions of billionaires and politicians or the tragic effects of climate change, and I've been looking for a way to give myself a bit of a buffer against the onslaught of despair. I finally found an effective method when I started using Filtre, a recently launched Safari extension that allows you to hide pages containing keywords you'd rather avoid. The concept is simple—create your list of keywords, and the extension will handle the rest. It works quite well for the most part, and I recommend trying it out—your mental health will thank you.How to set up Filtre on all your Apple devices
    
            
            
                                        Credit: Pranay Parab
                    
    
Filtre works on the iPhone, iPad, and Mac, and a single purchase unlocks the extension's features across all three platforms. You can get started with the week-long free trial, after which the app costs $1.50/month, $9/year, or a one-time purchase fee of $28. Once you've made that decision, it's easy to set up. You fire up the app, give Filtre permission to access websites, and start adding keywords you want to block. Extensions like these aren't very useful if they don't sync filters across devices, and I'm glad to see that Filtre ships with iCloud sync compatibility. In my testing, it was able to instantly sync filters between my Mac and iPhone.
Use this extension to filter out noise or avoid spoilersThere are many ways to use an extension like Filtre, but I've mostly been using it to limit political news. It's easy to set up a few keywords that block out names of politicians, and the extension lets you choose how you wish to obscure those headlines. The default option will hide all mentions of your chosen keywords, but you can also choose "Fade" or "Greyscale." Fade keeps headlines intact but blocks your keywords in place, and reduces the headlines' opacity to make them easier to skim past. Grayscale converts any part of the webpage that includes your keywords into black-and-white. You can adjust these options under the Default Settings tab in the Filtre app (which will appear in the app drawer on your iPhone/iPad or be searchable via Finder on a Mac).
    
            
            
                                        Credit: Pranay Parab
                    
    
Set different customizationsWhile universal filters are the default, you can also create different sets of filters for different websites. For instance, if you visit a website that covers movies or video games, you could create a filter set to hide spoilers from Christopher Nolan's Odyssey or Grand Theft Auto 6 on those sites only. 
            
                What do you think so far?
            
        
In Filtre's Website Settings tab, you'll be able to set up different filters for each site you frequent, or choose to disable some filter sets on certain sites. The extension allows you to export filter sets easily, which is convenient when you want to share your blocklists with others, and also for those who want to avoid using iCloud sync. You can send a filter list via AirDrop and load it on another of your devices.I did notice a few minor limitations while using the extension. For example, I've deleted Instagram's app from my devices, so when I need to access the site, I open it in Safari. Filtre didn't seem to have any impact on my Instagram feed at all. Since most social media sites allow you to mute keywords already, this limitation didn't bother me much. (On the plus side, Filtre does work with Reddit, though it started hiding my entire feed when I switched to Old Reddit.)Filtre passes the privacy checkMy biggest concern with browser extensions is how much data they can access. For Filtre to be effective across the web, you'll need to give it permission to access every website you visit, which might sound quite invasive. However, the developer Jeffrey Kuiken has explicitly stated that the app doesn't collect any of your data, and it even stores all your filters only locally and in the linked iCloud account. The same developer also made Noir, an extension that enables dark mode for every website you visit in Safari, and has earned the community's trust via a transparent business model and regular updates.

schema.headline: This Safari Extension Can Block Unwanted Content on Any Website

title: Samsung Just Announced Five New Products at Galaxy Unpacked
body:
Sachin Bahal
                            
        
            Sachin Bahal
                
                                                                        Freelance Writer
                                                            
            
            
        
            ExperienceSachin Bahal is a freelance tech writer. He has a bachelor's in journalism from Toronto Metropolitan University and studied journalism and photography at Durham College. A tech geek since childhood, Sachin started his own blog, TheCanadianTechie, in 2012. Since then, he has written hundreds of articles and reviews about smartphones, tablets, game consoles, wearables, and other consumer tech for sites like Tech Advisor, Digital Trends, and Console Creatures. 
        
        
            Areas of Expertise
        
        Read Full Bio
    

    
                                        
        
        
        July 22, 2026
                
        Add as a preferred source on Google

    
    
        Add as a preferred source on Google

            Credit: Sachin Bahal
                            
            
                Key Takeaways
                                            Samsung just announced a number of new products at Galaxy Unpacked.
                                            There are the foldables, like the Galaxy Z Fold 8 and Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra, as well as the Galaxy Z Flip 8.
                                            Samsung also announced the new Galaxy Watch 9 series, as well as the Galaxy Watch Ultra 2.
                                    
            
    Table of Contents
    
        
        Samsung's July Unpacked event is officially underway. Unlike its February event, which focused on Samsung's flagship smartphones, this event highlights the company's new foldables and smartwatches. As it happens, Samsung announced a total of five new products today, including one new Galaxy Z Flip, two new Galaxy Z Folds, and two new Galaxy watches. Here's everything the company revealed today at Unpacked:  The Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 introduces a new form factor
    
            
            
                                        Credit: Sachin Bahal
                    
    
Samsung's Galaxy Z Fold 8 has a new form factor this year. Instead of the usual tall book-style folding phone (which is now the Fold 8 Ultra), this foldable has a wider design that makes it feel like you're holding a small tablet when unfolded. The Z Fold 8 is just 9.7mm thick when folded and 4.5mm thick when unfolded. The phone is fairly light, and weighs 7.09 ounces (201g); for comparison, the Galaxy S26 Ultra is 7.54 ounces (214g). It's also IP48 water and dust-resistant. The Z Fold 8 opens up into a 7.6-inch display, while the cover screen is 5.5 inches. Both displays use Dynamic AMOLED 2X technology, similar to the Galaxy S26 series. Powering the Galaxy Z Fold 8 is the Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Elite 5 for Galaxy chip with 12GB or 16GB of RAM depending on storage capacity. Storage options include 256GB, 512GB, and 1TB. The battery in the Z Fold 8 is 4,800 mAh, which supports 45W Super Fast Wired charging and 20W wireless charging.
Unfortunately, the Fold 8 does not support Qi2, a faster wireless charging standard than Qi. There is a workaround, however, if you buy one of Samsung's official cases with Qi2 magnets built in. On top of that, just like the Fold 7, the Fold 8 has no S Pen support. For cameras, the Z Fold 8 has a dual-lens setup with a 50MP primary wide lens and a 50MP Ultra Wide lens. The front-facing cameras are 10MP. The Galaxy Z Fold 8 will be available in a few colors including Lavender, Graphite, and Cream. The Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 is available to order today from Samsung's website.The Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra succeeds the Z Fold 7
    
            
            
                                        Credit: Sachin Bahal
                    
    
The Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra is Samsung's successor to the Z Fold 7. Samsung made this foldable even thinner than before: It's now 4.1mm when unfolded, though it's still 8.9mm thick when folded up. The Z Fold 8 Ultra is also a tad lighter at 7.58 ounces (215g) compared to the Fold 7's 7.62 ounces (216g).The Z Fold 8 Ultra has an 8-inch main folding screen and 6.5-inch cover screen, both of which are Dynamic AMOLED 2X displays. The phone is also IP48 water and dust-resistant. Like the Z Fold 8, the Z Fold 8 Ultra comes with the Snapdragon 8 Elite 5 for Galaxy chip with either 12GB or 16GB of RAM. Similarly, you can choose from 256GB, 512GB, and 1TB models. The battery gets a bump this year, as it is now 5,000 mAh and supports both 45W Super Fast Wired charging and 25W wireless charging. Like the Fold 8, the Z Fold 8 Ultra also does not support Qi2, unless you buy an official Samsung case with magnets built in. It also doesn't support the S Pen.The Z Fold 8 Ultra retains the 200MP wide lens and 10MP telephoto from the Fold 7, but the Ultra Wide gets upgraded to 50MP. Both selfie cameras are 10MP, so not much changes there. You can get your Z Fold 8 Ultra in a few colors, including Violet Shadow, Graphite, and Cream. The Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra is available to order today from Samsung's website.Samsung keeps things mostly the same with the Galaxy Z Flip 8
    
            
            
                                        Credit: Sachin Bahal
                    
    
If you're into compact foldables, there's the Galaxy Z Flip 8, which gets some minor updates from last year's model. Samsung flipped back to a Qualcomm chip this year, as the Z Flip 8 also gets the Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 for Galaxy chip with 12GB of RAM.
            
                What do you think so far?
            
        
The screens on the Galaxy Z Flip 8 haven't changed; the FlexWindow cover screen is still 4.1 inches, but Samsung did reduce the bezels around the display. Samsung also added support for 16 different apps you can use on the FlexWindow, so you can check in with them without opening up the phone. Samsung didn't change the battery here, shipping the same 4,300 mAh unit as last year. The Z Flip 8 supports 25W wired and 15W wireless charging, and is IP48-rated for water and dust resistance. For cameras, there's a 50MP primary wide lens, a 12MP Ultra Wide, and a 10MP selfie camera. Storage options are limited to 256GB and 512GB. The Galaxy Z Flip 8 is available to order today from Samsung's website.Samsung introduces the Galaxy Watch 9 series
    
            
            
                                        Credit: Sachin Bahal
                    
    
Alongside the new foldables, Samsung also unveiled the Galaxy Watch 9 series. The Galaxy Watch 9 comes in two sizes: a 40mm model with a 1.3-inch screen, and a 44mm model with a 1.5-inch screen. Each has a peak brightness of 3,000 nits. Powering the watches is the Qualcomm SW6100 chip with 2GB of RAM and 32GB of storage. The watches are IP68 water and dust-resistant and can withstand 5ATM (five atmospheres of pressure). The smaller model comes with a 390 mAh battery, while the larger has a 445 mAh battery, and both models support fast charging. The 40mm comes in Cream and Graphite colors, and the 44mm will be available in Silver and Graphite. The Samsung Galaxy Watch 9 series is available to order today from Samsung's websiteSamsung's Galaxy Watch Ultra 2 is here
    
            
            
                                        Credit: Sachin Bahal
                    
    
If you're looking for a bit more from your smartwatch, there's the new Galaxy Watch Ultra 2. This device has a 1.52-inch display with a peak brightness of 5000 nits. The Galaxy Watch Ultra 2 has a rating of IP69K, and can survive up to 10ATM. Samsung says it can be used for ocean swimming and diving. Powering the Galaxy Watch Ultra 2 is the Qualcomm SW6100 chip with 2GB of RAM and 64GB of storage. The watch features an 800 mAh battery with fast charging support. The Samsung Galaxy Watch Ultra 2 is available to order today from Samsung's website.

schema.headline: Samsung Just Announced Five New Products at Galaxy Unpacked

title: Samsung's New Galaxy Smart Glasses Aim for Style and Google Integration
body:
Stephen Johnson
                            
        
            Stephen Johnson
                
                                            Senior Staff Writer
                                    
            
            
        
            ExperienceStephen Johnson is a senior staff writer at Lifehacker covering pop culture and technology, including the columns “The Out-of-Touch Adults’ Guide to Kid Culture” and “What People Are Getting Wrong This Week.”
        
        
            Areas of Expertise
        
        Read Full Bio
    

    
                                        
        
        
        July 22, 2026
                
        Add as a preferred source on Google

    
    
        Add as a preferred source on Google

            Credit: Samsung-YouTube
                            
            
                Key Takeaways
                                            Samsung released new details on its smart glasses at Samsung Unpacked.
                                            These AI-focused glasses run on Android's XR platform, feature Gemini Live AI, and are powered by Qualcomm's Snapdragon AR Gen 1 chip.
                                            The Galaxy Glasses are scheduled for release this fall, though the price has not been announced.
                                    
            
    Table of Contents
    
        
        At its Unpacked event in London this morning, Samsung closed its presentation with a surprise, dropping some details on its soon-to-be-released line of smart glasses. Designed in partnership with Warby Parker, Gentle Monster, and Google, Samsung's "Galaxy Glasses" (if that's what they wind up being called) are audio-, AI-, and camera-focused. Like Meta's Ray-Ban and Oakley glasses, these glasses will not have a display (although Samsung display glasses are reportedly in development), but they will be integrated into the Google ecosystem. The Galaxy Glasses (or whatever) will launch this fall in a variety of fashion-forward designs from Gentle Monster and Warby Parker. 
    
            
            
                                        Credit: Samsung-YouTube
                    
    
What we know about Samsung's line of smart glassesAll models of Samsung's upcoming smart glasses will all feature the same tech under the hood.
Operating system: Runs on Google's Android XR platform.AI agent: Integrated with Gemini Live AI.Processor: Powered by Qualcomm's Snapdragon AR 1 Gen 1 chip. Controls: Wear OS integration allows control from Galaxy WatchesBattery life: Up to nine hours of battery life per charge, plus seven full charges from the carrying case. Camera and audio: Front-facing camera with outward privacy LED indicator and with dual open-ear speakers.Samsung highlighted several practical use cases for the eyewear during the Unpacked presentation, including looking at a whiteboard to auto-save information into Samsung Notes, making restaurant reservations and coffee orders on the fly, and taking pictures and video by touching the frame.
            
                What do you think so far?
            
        
What we don't know yet about Samsung's smart glassesHere are some of the "missing pieces" about these glasses.The price: No information has been released about what they'll cost. Release date: While they are scheduled for "this fall," the exact release date has yet to be revealed.Camera features: Megapixel count, resolution, and field-of-view specs have yet to be specified. Offline capability: It's not clear how much AI processing will happen through the onboard Snapdragon chip, and how much will rely on a connection to the cloud/your phone.Samsung's entry into the display-free smart glasses space share a lot of features with Meta's line of smart glasses—both include onboard cameras, open-air speakers, and a heavy focus on AI—but there are some distinct differences as well. Here are some areas where Samsung aims to set itself apart from its primary competitor:The ecosystem: If you're already rocking a Samsung phone and Galaxy Watch, these glasses will lock right into your device setup. But the big selling point may turn out to be their utility with Google: Glasses that fully integrate with Google products means you'll be able to get instant Google Maps navigation, create events in your Google Calendar, use Google's live translation, use Google's Workplace apps, and more.The AI: While Meta's glasses are tied to Meta AI, Samsung's will use Google's Gemini Live. Whether that's a plus or a minus is a matter of  personal preference, of course. Design: For most of us, finding a pair of glasses that look good is at least as important as which AI agent they connect to. Samsung partnering with both a "trendy" eyewear company like Gentle Monster and a more everyday company like Warby Parker promises to give you more choices, so you can find a pair that's flattering instead of making do with the clunky default option.
            
    
        
                
                    
                Lifehacker has been a go-to source of tech help and life advice since 2005. Our mission is to offer reliable tech help and credible, practical, science-based life advice to help you live better.
                
                
                    
                        Our Mission
                        Our Team
                        Newsletter

                Lifehacker is a federally registered trademark of Ziff Davis and may not be used by third parties without explicit permission. The display of third-party trademarks and trade names on this site does not necessarily indicate

                    endorsement of Lifehacker. If you click an affiliate link and buy a product or service, we may be paid a fee by that merchant.

title: How Garmin's Acquisition of TrainingPeaks Might Affect Your Fitness Routine
body:
Meredith Dietz
                            
        
            Meredith Dietz
                
                                            Senior Staff Writer
                                    
            
            
        
            ExperienceMeredith is a marathon runner and stand-up comedian. As Lifehacker’s Senior Staff Writer, she covers personal fitness tech, home gym equipment, and more.
        
        
            Areas of Expertise
        
        Read Full Bio
    

    
                                        
        
        
        July 22, 2026
                
        Add as a preferred source on Google

    
    
        Add as a preferred source on Google

            Credit: Meredith Dietz
                            
    Table of Contents
    
        
        This morning, Garmin announced it has acquired TrainingPeaks and TrainHeroic. These platforms are major players in the endurance and strength-training world, primarily for the quality of their training plans. This news is already sparking a lot of speculation—and skepticism—among athletes and coaches who've built their training around these tools. Here's what this acquisition might mean for the way you train.Why Garmin acquired TrainingPeaksAccording to Garmin's official press release, this acquisition “strengthens Garmin's fitness ecosystem with customizable coaching experiences for endurance, strength, and performance-focused athletes across every stage of their fitness journey.” The deal covers TrainingPeaks and TrainHeroic only, not all of parent company Peaksware Holdings. This is a Garmin-specific focus on its endurance and strength assets, rather than a sale of the entire parent company.TrainingPeaks is best known for its training plans, logs, and coaching tools, but this deal is drawing attention to other features and perks. For instance, Garmin likely has its eye on TrainingPeaks Virtual, a nifty indoor cycling platform. 
It's also worth noting that TrainingPeaks owns the trademarks behind some popular metrics for serious cyclists, like Training Stress Score, Normalized Power, and Intensity Factor. As of right now, other companies like Wahoo, Hammerhead, and TrainerRoad license those terms for use in their own products—we'll see if the Garmin deal impacts this. Garmin already dominates the hardware layer with its watches, bike computers, and sensors that generate the data. What it hasn't dominated is the planning and coaching layer that sits on top of that.There's also a strength-training angle with TrainHeroic. Garmin is clearly trying to get firmer footing in the strength and hybrid training worlds, and buying up an established strength-programming platform is a much faster path than building one from scratch.What happens to TrainingPeaks now?This is where the community's nervousness comes in, and it's not unfounded. As of right now, TrainingPeaks and TrainHeroic connects to more than 100 devices and apps, including hardware from Garmin's direct competitors like Wahoo, Coros, Polar, Suunto, and Apple. Garmin now has incentive to make that platform less accessible. Whether it acts on that incentive is unclear, so there are three broad scenarios people are speculating about:Best case: Garmin leaves TrainingPeaks largely alone as a standalone product, keeps it multi-brand, and maybe integrates some select TrainingPeaks features into Connect+.Middle case: Garmin treats TrainingPeaks the way Strava has treated Runna since acquiring it, which might look like a sort of combo-deal subscription.Worst case: Garmin absorbs TrainingPeaks' features and utterly paywalls them. Existing users lose years of workout history, race notes, and coaching plans.Nothing in today's announcement addresses these fears directly. For now, Garmin's messaging is all about "enhancing the ecosystem." 
            
                What do you think so far?
            
        
The bottom lineAcquisitions like this typically take months (sometimes longer) before any product changes roll out, and Garmin has given no timeline. Here are some longer-term questions worth watching:Will non-Garmin device support go away? Will Garmin's strength training finally get better? Will pricing for either platform change? Garmin is already one of the biggest names in athlete devices and data; today's acquisition expands the company's reach more into coaching and structured training plans. Maybe TrainingPeaks' best training tools make it into Connect+, Garmin's strength training gets a long-overdue upgrade, and everybody wins. Or maybe a beloved independent tool gets slowly absorbed and degraded. For now, all we can do is keep an eye on whether TrainingPeaks' device integrations with Garmin competitors stay intact, and what happens to pricing.

schema.headline: How Garmin's Acquisition of TrainingPeaks Might Affect Your Fitness Routine

title: How to Preorder the Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8, Z Fold 8 Ultra, or Z Flip 8
body:
Jake Peterson
                            
        
            Jake Peterson
                
                                            Senior Technology Editor
                                    
            
            
        
            ExperienceJake Peterson is Lifehacker’s Tech Editor, and has been covering tech news and how-tos for nearly a decade. His team covers all things technology, including AI, smartphones, computers, game consoles, and subscriptions.
        
        
            Areas of Expertise
        
        Read Full Bio
    

    
                                        
        
        
        July 22, 2026
                
        Add as a preferred source on Google

    
    
        Add as a preferred source on Google

    We may earn a commission from links on this page.
    
            Credit: Samsung
                            
            
                Key Takeaways
                                            Samsung just announced the Galaxy Z Fold 8, Z Fold 8 Ultra, and Z Flip 8 at its July Unpacked event.
                                            You can preorder all three devices today from Samsung's website.
                                            The devices will officially be available starting Aug. 7.
                                    
            
    Table of Contents
    
        
        During its July Unpacked event, Samsung officially unveiled its next-generation of foldable Galaxy devices. There's the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra, which, despite the name, is really the successor to the Galaxy Z Fold 7. The Galaxy Z Fold 8, instead, is actually a new device, introducing a lighter design (and a slightly smaller price tag) than the Z Fold 8 Ultra. Finally, there's the Z Flip 8, which actually doesn't change much from the Z Flip 7.  If you're in the market for a new foldable smartphone, these new devices might be on your radar. Even the Z Flip 8's minor adjustments might prove a worthwhile upgrade if your Flip device is more than a few years old. While you can't buy one today, you can preorder any of them right now. The Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra is Samsung's premium foldable for 2026. Out of Samsung's newest products, it has the biggest and best cameras (200MP Wide, 50MP Ultra Wide, and 10MP Tele), the largest displays (6.5-inch outer display, 8-inch inner display), the biggest battery (5,000 mAh), and, unfortunately, comes with the biggest price tag. If you're interested, you can preorder from the following links:  
Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra (256 GB): $2,099.99 at SamsungSamsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra (512 GB): $2,299.99 at SamsungSamsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra (1TB): $2,699.99 at SamsungHow to preorder the Galaxy Z Fold 8 A lot of people are going to think the Z Fold 8 is the upgraded version of the Z Fold 7, but it's not. This is a lighter take on Samsung's foldable, which might appeal to users who want a phablet that takes up a little less space in their pocket. It comes with a 5.5-inch outer display, a 7.6-inch inner display, three rear cameras (50MP Wide, 50MP Ultra Wide, and 2x Optical Zoom), and the same Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 chip as the rest of the "Z 8" line. With that lighter framework comes a slightly smaller MSRP (emphasis on slightly), as the Z Fold 8 starts at $1,899.99. Here are the preorder links from Samsung:
            
                What do you think so far?
            
        
Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 (256 GB): $1,899.99 at SamsungSamsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 (512 GB): $2,099.99 at SamsungSamsung Galaxy Z Flip 8 (1TB): $2,499.99 at SamsungHow to preorder the Galaxy Z Flip 8The Z Flip 8 isn't much changed from the Z Flip 7. It comes with a 4.1-inch outer display and a 6.9-inch inner display (though the peak brightness increases to 2600 nits from 1600 nits), 12GB of RAM, and similar cameras to the Z Flip 7, including a 50MP Wide, 12MP Ultra Wide, and 10X Digital Zoom. (That said, it does have a new 2x optical zoom.) It also upgrades the SoC from the Exynos 2500  As such, this isn't the device you upgrade to if you bought Samsung's flip phone last year. Still, it's the company's flagship Z Flip, so if you're looking to upgrade, or thinking about picking up a flip phone for the first time, it's one to consider. Here's where to preorder: Samsung Galaxy Z Flip 8 (256 GB): $1,199.99 at SamsungSamsung Galaxy Z Flip 8 (512GB): $1,399.99 at Samsung

schema.headline: How to Preorder the Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8, Z Fold 8 Ultra, or Z Flip 8

title: Roborock's New LiDAR Robot Lawn Mower Is Already $700 Off
description:
Add as a preferred source on Google

    We may earn a commission from links on this page.
            Deal pricing and availability sub
body:
Add as a preferred source on Google

    We may earn a commission from links on this page.
            Deal pricing and availability subject to change after time of publication.
    
            Credit: Amazon
                            
    Table of Contents
    
        
        Roborock threw its hat into the wireless robot lawn mower game this month with the AI-powered Roborock X120H robot lawn mower. If you’re still spending precious time pushing a mower around on hot days, you might want to consider this convenient home automation device, especially given its launch-period sale, which brings the price down by around $700 to $2,499.99, originally $3,199.
It can mow up to half an acre and uses a combination of LiDAR and AI vision navigation, AWD, active steering, a high cutting range, and a Dynamic Suspension System, making it a strong option for difficult or uneven terrain. There’s no need for a perimeter wire or RTK antenna, thanks to its 360-degree LiDAR scanner and binocular vision, which can also help with navigation under trees. It has AWD (aka, four-wheel drive), active front-wheel steering, and it can tackle slopes up to 38.7 degrees and obstacles up to 3.1 inches.It has knobby wheels and comes with six blades with a cutting-height range of 1.57 to 3.54 inches, which is useful for both weekly maintenance and for those who keep a taller lawn. It has a 9.45-inch cutting width, plus a rain sensor and an IPX6 rating, meaning it can be hosed down after use. When it detects rain, it returns to its charging station and resumes mowing after its rain-delay period. Like most robot mowers, it can be controlled through the Roborock app. It also includes three years of free 4G connectivity for remote tracking, along with a PIN- and GPS-based anti-theft system.
            
                What do you think so far?
            
        
There are still relatively few robot mowers that use LiDAR, so if you have uneven or mixed terrain, this model is a good choice. LiDAR also helps it navigate in low light, cluttered yards, and more complex layouts. Combined with AWD and its high-cut capability, the Roborock X120H robot lawn mower is a promising option for small to medium lawns.
            
                    Our Best Editor-Vetted Tech Deals Right Now
                
            Deals are selected by our commerce team

title: These Gboard Alternatives Are Open-Source and Privacy-First
body:
Khamosh Pathak
                            
        
            Khamosh Pathak
                
                                                                        Freelance Writer
                                                            
            
            
        
            ExperienceKhamosh Pathak is a freelance tech journalist with over 13 years of experience writing online. An accounting graduate, he turned his interest in writing and technology into a career. He holds a specialization in user experience design from ISDI Parsons in Mumbai, where he sought to deepen his understanding of UX, consumer devices, and interplay between software and technology.Khamosh has written thousands of how-tos and guides on every major platform (iOS, macOS, Android, and Windows). He got started writing how-tos and guides for Guiding Tech, where he also published several e-books. His work has also been seen at MakeUseOf, How-To Geek, and PCMag. He is truly passionate about brewing good coffee.
        
        
            Areas of Expertise
        
        Read Full Bio
    

    
                                        
        
        
        July 16, 2026
                
        Add as a preferred source on Google

    
    
        Add as a preferred source on Google

            Credit: Igor Suka/Getty Images
                            
    Table of Contents
    
        
        Gboard is the default keyboard for Pixel smartphones and many devices that use stock Android, but it’s not the only option out there. Recently, Gboard has been swept up in a privacy controversy highlighting how your data is used by Google for its own training and collection. The data that Google collects can be intercepted and tied to individual users. Luckily, you can disable some of those data-sharing settings. But what if, down the line, Gboard changes its policy or privacy settings? Or we learn about additional ways Google sneakily collects your data from your keyboard? If that concerns you, consider using an open-source keyboard that runs completely on device, no data servers required. Unlike closed software, security experts can audit open-source programs, so vulnerabilities are found and patched quite quickly. As it happens, there are two solid open-source alternatives to Gboard. One is so like Gboard, you won’t even miss it. The second is as private as a keyboard gets.FUTO Keyboard is a direct Gboard replacement
    
            
            
                                        Credit: Khamosh Pathak
                    
    
FUTO Keyboard is designed to be a direct Gboard replacement. It looks like Gboard, and it works like Gboard. But underneath, it’s completely open-source, free, and private. By default, none of your keystrokes leave your device.
FUTO includes on-device AI models for text prediction and voice typing. The text prediction model works completely offline and doesn't use any online servers. In my testing, it kept up with my typing, and the suggestions were more or less what you'd expect. For voice typing, the app uses a lightweight Whisper-based model, also running on-device, with no servers involved. In my testing, I found the default model to be a bit underwhelming when compared to Gboard. Downloading the highest-tier model resolved this issue, though, and I saw voice typing results similar to Gboard. (The English-244 voice model is a 280MB download.)
    
            
            
                                        Credit: Khamosh Pathak
                    
    
FUTO gets a lot more fun when you start customizing the settings and the layout. You can resize the keyboard to make it larger, add a permanent numbers row to the top, an arrow bar, and change the theme to your liking. There are also fine-tuning settings for users who like adjusting every bit of how their keyboard works. For example, there is a section on Long-Press Keys that lets you customize how the backspace long-press gesture works.In my testing, I found that the FUTO keyboard offers a smoother, more reliable typing experience compared to Gboard. A small example is the spacebar gesture. In Gboard, you can move the cursor by swiping on the spacebar, but it’s a bit confusing for me. You hold it for a split second, and then swipe. In FUTO, swiping on the spacebar instantly moves the cursor, every single time. The downside to being completely private is, of course, that you lose out on cross-device support. Your personal dictionary, or clipboard, can’t sync between all your devices. While Gboard is a more complete package, FUTO's privacy features do make up for that loss.HeliBoard is the Gboard alternative for security purists
    
            
            
                                        Credit: Khamosh Pathak
                    
    
HeliBoard has a rich open-source pedigree. It’s based on the old OpenBoard project, which itself was based on the pure Android AOSP keyboard, the original stock Android keyboard.
            
                What do you think so far?
            
        
The times have changed, though, and HeliBoard has kept up, without sacrificing its open-source roots. The app promises a zero-exposure experience, asking for no network permissions whatsoever. As this is a purist’s keyboard, there are no AI features to speak of: no LLM transformer for text prediction, no AI voice typing model.That said, all the basic features you’d expect from an Android keyboard are all here. You can add an optional number row to the top, and there’s support for gesture typing, along with an on-device clipboard (which can be disabled). You can customize the appearance as well. You can resize the keyboard, bring back the old Holo keyboard style, change icons, and change the color of the keyboard. (You can even use an image as the background.)The only caveat to using HeliBoard is that it’s not available in the Google Play Store. Instead, you’ll have to download it from the F-Droid third-party app store that specializes in securely hosting free and open-source apps. But that’s easy to do, and once installed, you can explore other useful open-source apps like NewPipe, my preferred way of watching YouTube ad-free on Android.

schema.headline: These Gboard Alternatives Are Open-Source and Privacy-First

title: This Free Mac App Can Explain Why Your USB-C Cable Is Charging So Slowly
body:
David Nield
                            
        
            David Nield
                
                                                                        Freelance Writer
                                                            
            
            
        
            ExperienceDavid Nield is a technology journalist from Manchester in the U.K. who has been writing about gadgets and apps for more than 20 years. He has a bachelor's degree in English Literature from Durham University, where he also spent a term as editor of the award-winning student newspaper Palatinate. His journalism career started in print media, where he contributed to and edited several technology magazines and bookazines sold in the U.K. and internationally. More recently, he has worked as a freelancer for some of the biggest technology publications on the web, covering everything from on-the-ground reporting about product launches, to detailed explainers and how-to guides on apps, gadgets, and platforms. His expertise covers broad areas of consumer tech, including smartphones, laptops, wearables, and AI.
        
        
            Areas of Expertise
        
        Read Full Bio
    

    
                                        
        
        
        July 13, 2026
                
        Add as a preferred source on Google

    
    
        Add as a preferred source on Google

            Credit: vshtun/Shutterstock 
                            
    Table of Contents
    
        
        Understanding cable tech can be hard at times—harder than it should be, really—and weighing the pros and cons of a new gadget is complicated enough without having to know the differences between HDMI 2.1 and HDMI 2.2, or the reason some USB-C ports are also Thunderbolt ports. Help is at hand, though: WhatCable is a free app that does a fine job of analyzing the cables attached to your Mac—not just giving you a list of specs but also explaining what those specs mean. One way it can be useful is in explaining why your MacBook isn't charging as quickly as it could be. This can be down to the charger and the specific USB-C cable you've got connected, and WhatCable will break all of this down for you without any jargon. You'll see the rate your laptop is charging at, and the reasons why.Why your cables matterTo fully explain the modern cable landscape would take an article many times the length of this one, but it is possible to get to grips with the basics relatively quickly. Every connection has three elements that you need to consider together: The port on the device you're connecting to (like a monitor or charger), the cable in between, and the port on the device you're using (like a phone or laptop).
For the best results (the fastest charging or the smoothest display performance, for example), all those elements must be supporting the same standards. If they aren't, you might not get optimum results, or the setup might not work at all.Unfortunately for us as consumers, simply finding a cable that fits a port isn't enough to guarantee everything will work, or work as well as you might like. Both cables and ports come with supported standards that you need to weigh, like the various flavors of USB (we're up to USB 4.0 version 2 now, for reference).
    
            
            Always double-check the specs when buying a new cable.
                                        Credit: Lifehacker
                    
    
The most common port you'll see on laptops and phones today is USB-C, but this is only the start of the story. USB-C ports can also support Thunderbolt and DisplayPort protocols, as well as a variety of USB speeds—you need to check the device spec for details. Even similar-sized ports on the same device may be configured differently.When you've determined what the ports on your computer or phone are capable of, you need to find a cable that supports the same standard to get the best possible results. Be careful when reviewing cable listings before buying, both in terms of specs and length—cables beyond one meter (a little over three feet) typically require extra tech to support the highest data speeds, and will therefore usually be more expensive.To add to the confusion, these standards can change pretty regularly, with manufacturers sometimes adopting the changes promptly and sometimes waiting a while to implement them. The short version is, don't rush cable buying, or think that all cables and ports are the same. Spend a few extra minutes analyzing the relevant specs in detail, and it'll pay off.
            
                What do you think so far?
            
        
How WhatCable can help figure out your cablesYou can download WhatCable for free from its website or GitHub page, which will both direct you to a zip archive. Launch the app, and WhatCable shows up on the menu bar; click its icon to see details of connected USB cables. Via the cog icon (top right) you can have WhatCable launch with macOS, and run as a regular app rather than from the menu bar. As soon as you connect a cable, you'll see information on the charging speed and data transfer rate (where applicable), and a breakdown of what the cable can do.If you've connected a charger, then you'll be told whether or not it's a good match for your MacBook. Look for the "charging well" message for confirmation, alongside the charging rate. If a cable isn't charging your MacBook at the maximum speed, or the MacBook is itself limiting the charging (because the battery is almost full), you'll be told about this too.
    
            
            WhatCable presents its data in a simple, understandable way.
                                        Credit: Lifehacker
                    
    
If you've hooked up a phone or another peripheral, then its identity will be reported inside WhatCable, and it's here that the data transfer speeds might be more relevant. If an external storage device has been connected, then you'll see the transfer speed it's negotiated with the Apple operating system.WhatCable also looks at the e-marker inside a cable, which is essentially its digital ID, advertising its capabilities to the computer it's plugged into. If there are discrepancies between this e-marker and commonly followed technology standards, then you'll see an orange flag. It's not necessarily saying the cable is a fake, but just alerting you to something that doesn't quite seem right.In short, any details that the cable is reporting to macOS will be shown by WhatCable, and you should see a significant difference between cheap and limited cables and the more expensive and powerful ones—which will likely be reassuring if you've paid extra for them.

schema.headline: This Free Mac App Can Explain Why Your USB-C Cable Is Charging So Slowly

title: This Android App Blocks the Most Addicting Parts of Social Media
body:
Khamosh Pathak
                            
        
            Khamosh Pathak
                
                                                                        Freelance Writer
                                                            
            
            
        
            ExperienceKhamosh Pathak is a freelance tech journalist with over 13 years of experience writing online. An accounting graduate, he turned his interest in writing and technology into a career. He holds a specialization in user experience design from ISDI Parsons in Mumbai, where he sought to deepen his understanding of UX, consumer devices, and interplay between software and technology.Khamosh has written thousands of how-tos and guides on every major platform (iOS, macOS, Android, and Windows). He got started writing how-tos and guides for Guiding Tech, where he also published several e-books. His work has also been seen at MakeUseOf, How-To Geek, and PCMag. He is truly passionate about brewing good coffee.
        
        
            Areas of Expertise
        
        Read Full Bio
    

    
                                        
        
        
        July 20, 2026
                
        Add as a preferred source on Google

    
    
        Add as a preferred source on Google

            Credit: Khamosh Pathak
                            
    Table of Contents
    
        
        I open Instagram to check out my friends' Stories, updates from my local community, and the latest dog video my wife sent me last night. But after doing all of that, I find myself doomscrolling for hours. It got so bad, I ended up deleting the app and using the website exclusively. It's a similar story on YouTube, where I end up scrolling through endless Shorts. When YouTube’s Shorts limit came out, I rushed to enable it.But I’ve found a better solution for both apps, at least for my Pixel. There’s a setting hidden deep inside the Switchly app that blocks certain addicting elements of popular apps. Switchly is completely free, open-source, and doesn’t show ads: Its main job is to serve as a free and open-source alternative to devices like Brick. It blocks apps and websites to help you focus, and the only way to unblock is by using an NFC tag and a QR code—the Android equivalent of the Foqos iPhone app. Here's how it works. Switchly can block reels, the Explore page, comments, and Stories on Instagram. On YouTube, it can block Shorts, the Subscriptions page, the "You" tab (YouTube's recommendation feed), and picture-in-picture mode. On X, it can block the home page, search, Grok AI, or notifications. On Snapchat, you can disable the Map, Stories, Spotlight, or the Following page. You can really disable the features that distract you the most. For me, that’s reels, search, and comments on Instagram, and Shorts and the You page on YouTube.
Switchly is an app blocker, so it needs accessibility and screen access permissions before it can work. Because it’s a free and open-source app, with code hosted on GitLab, third-party testers can verify Switchly's security, so I find it safe to use.
            
                What do you think so far?
            
        

    
            
            
                                        Credit: Khamosh Pathak
                    
    
Once you grant the app the needed permissions, go to the Blocking tab, then go to the In-app blocking section. Here, enable the features you want to block in each app (this will only work if Switchly’s global blocking is currently disabled). After that, go to the Home page, and from the top, tap Enable. The blocking will now be enabled until you disable it manually.
    
            
            
                                        Credit: Khamosh Pathak
                    
    
If, like me, you want to ban these features forever, you can leave it at that. But if you only want to disable them during work hours or on weekdays, you can set up a schedule. If you would like to block certain apps and websites, you can add them from the Manage Apps and Blocked Websites sections. To set up a schedule, go to Manage > Schedules, tap the Plus button, create a new schedule, and customize it to your needs. As we discussed earlier, the app offers many more ruthless options for controlling the block. You can go to Blocking > Blocking Controls to switch to a different blocking method.

schema.headline: This Android App Blocks the Most Addicting Parts of Social Media

title: This Period-Tracking App Actually Keeps Your Data Private
body:
Beth Skwarecki
                            
        
            Beth Skwarecki
                
                                            Senior Health Editor
                                    
            
            
        
            ExperienceBeth Skwarecki is Lifehacker’s Senior Health Editor, and holds certifications as a personal trainer and weightlifting coach. She has been writing about health for over 10 years.
        
        
            Areas of Expertise
        
        Read Full Bio
    

    
                                        
        
        
        July 20, 2026
                
        Add as a preferred source on Google

    
    
        Add as a preferred source on Google

    We may earn a commission from links on this page.
    
            Credit: Euki
                            
    Table of Contents
    
        
        Period tracking is a simple job that plenty of apps can do. But most apps these days—including those that track health data—store information about you on their own servers and sometimes even share that data with advertisers. The Mozilla Foundation has tested several of the most popular period-tracking apps, and it identified one as better than the rest at keeping your data private: Euki. Privacy is especially important for period trackers in light of the political and legal battles that surround abortion and birth control. We know that police have used other data, like messages and search history, to prosecute people for alleged abortions. It’s understandable if you don’t want tech companies to keep or share data about your reproductive health. Our sister site PCMag has a guide here to tracking your period privately. They recommend Apple Health or DIY methods like coded notes on a calendar.Recently, the Mozilla Foundation did a privacy audit of six of the top period tracking apps. It found serious privacy flaws in Stardust, SpotOn, Period Calendar, Flo, and Clue, but held up Euki as the best of the bunch: “We strongly recommend Euki for anyone who puts privacy first," it said.
How to install and use Euki privatelyEuki is available for both iOS and Android. The app is free, with no in-app purchases, and the Apple App Store reports that the developer does not collect any data from the app. You don’t need to create an account to use the app; it starts working as soon as you install it.Most importantly, your data is stored locally on your phone and not sent to any servers. Most period-tracking apps, including the more privacy-focused ones that say they encrypt or anonymize your data, still store your data on servers that the company has access to. Some say they would fight a subpoena if they were asked to hand over data; but it’s probably better not to give your data to a company in the first place. 
            
                What do you think so far?
            
        
Euki has a few other privacy features. You can set a PIN when you install the app, and a fake PIN that will hide your data. (When I enter the fake PIN, I get a message that the app is having technical difficulties and I should try again later.) You can tell Euki to delete your previous data, either by tapping a button in settings or by setting up a schedule to regularly delete old data. As for the app’s features, you can log just about anything to a given day. There are categories for bleeding, emotions, body symptoms, sexual activity, and more. You can track appointments and even things like when you check the strings on your IUD. 
            
    
        
                
                    
                Lifehacker has been a go-to source of tech help and life advice since 2005. Our mission is to offer reliable tech help and credible, practical, science-based life advice to help you live better.

schema.headline: This Period-Tracking App Actually Keeps Your Data Private
